game_name: Fire Emblem: Genealogy of the Holy War

design_philosophy.core_loop: Turn-based tactical positioning and combat on grid maps with permanent character consequences, relationship building through support conversations, and generational narrative progression across two linked story arcs
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward of unit positioning and tactics against permanent death mechanic; narrative tension of character relationships influencing gameplay outcomes across generations
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a growing army of diverse warriors with intertwined fates, forge relationships that span generations, and experience a complex political/military saga where character choices matter permanently
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game prioritizes narrative weight and character consequence over player save-scumming comfort; introduces romance and support systems as core mechanics affecting gameplay stats; generational progression mechanic ties player decisions in Part 1 to Part 2 character effectiveness; permanent death rule is non-negotiable except for plot-protected characters

difficulty.lives_system: Permanent death for all non-plot-protected units; army leader death or home castle seizure forces map restart
difficulty.continues_system: Save at start of each turn; map restart on failure
difficulty.checkpointing: Turn-by-turn save system allows granular checkpoint placement
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High - player unit positioning, weapon triangle exploitation, and support relationship management directly determine survival and effectiveness; permanent death consequences force strategic thinking rather than reflexive action

progression.upgrade_system: Random stat growth on level-up (health, strength, speed, etc.); weapon upgrades through purchases and repairs at base/castle towns; second-generation unit stats influenced by parent attribute assignments
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story chapters unlock new maps and units sequentially; generational structure locks Part 1 completion before Part 2 access; character availability tied to narrative events and recruitment
replayability_hooks: Second-generation stat inheritance varies based on first-generation parental pairings and attribute assignments
Support conversations and romance options available through repeated playthroughs create different relationship outcomes
Arena battles optional for grinding and experimentation
Unit survival variations across playthroughs due to permanent death mechanic

world_building_techniques: Two-generation storyline structure (Sigurd's rise and fall in Part 1, Seliph's redemption in Part 2) showing cyclical history
Eight-nation continent (Jugdral) with distinct political factions founded by Twelve Crusaders mythology
Character relationship conversations integrated into mission downtime via support system
Story-driven character romances that trigger during campaign progression
Plot-critical characters embedded in gameplay decisions and map control objectives
Scripted narrative escalation: seizing castles triggers enemy reinforcements and objective changes within maps
Cult antagonist (Loptous revival) provides overarching threat across both generations

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters integrated into narrative progression; boss units often reflect faction leadership and ideological conflict; player relationships and stat inheritance influence boss difficulty perception
notable_bosses: Faction leaders tied to Crusader bloodlines
Cult servants working to revive Loptous
Generational echoes: antagonists in Part 1 influence Part 2 dynamics

faction_indicators: Eight founding nations with distinct military cultures and Crusader heritage
Cult of Loptous as unifying antagonistic faction
Character bloodline affiliation determines class availability and personal skills
Support relationships create inter-faction bonds overriding military allegiance
danger_signals: Permanent unit death creates genuine stakes and emotional investment loss
Home castle seizure as map failure condition shows vulnerability of player base
Leadership rank system concentrates power in key units, creating targeted threat zones
Second-generation dependency on first-generation decisions creates generational vulnerability
environmental_storytelling: Castle towns serve dual purpose: mechanical hubs and narrative anchors showing civilian life during war
Map objectives (castle capture, defense) tied to faction control and territorial dominance
Weapon Triangle system reflects mythological balance (lance > sword > axe > lance) creating tactical philosophy
Character placement restrictions based on class and relationship (married units benefit from proximity) show social bonds in mechanical form

sources_used: wikipedia - Fire Emblem: Genealogy of the Holy War
